elements.sent-2411-001: We present an algorithm for the efficient generation of all pairwise non-isomorphic cycle permutation graphs, i.e.\ cubic graphs with a $2$-factor consisting of two chordless cycles, and non-hamiltonian cycle permutation graphs, from which the permutation snarks can easily be computed.
elements.sent-2411-002: This allows us to generate all cycle permutation graphs up to order $34$ and all permutation snarks up to order $46$, improving upon previous computational results by Brinkmann et al. Moreover, we give several improved lower bounds for interesting permutation snarks, such as for a smallest permutation snark of order $6\bmod 8$ or a smallest permutation snark of girth at least $6$.
elements.sent-2411-003: These computational results also allow us to complete a characterisation of the orders for which non-hamiltonian cycle permutation graphs exist, answering an open question by Klee from 1972, and yield many more counterexamples to a conjecture by Zhang.. Keywords.
elements.sent-2411-004: Cycle permutation graphs, Permutation snarks, Exhaustive generation, Canonical construction path method, Orderly generation, Non-hamiltonian \end{minipage \end{center.}}
elements.sent-2411-005: A cycle permutation graph is a cubic graph containing a $2$-factor consisting of two chordless cycles.
elements.sent-2411-006: One can easily see that both of these cycles must have length $n/2$, where $n$ is the order of the graph.
elements.sent-2411-007: Cycle permutation graphs were first introduced by Chartrand and Harary~\cite{CH67 in 1967.}
elements.sent-2411-008: They characterised the conditions under which a cycle permutation graph is planar.
elements.sent-2411-009: In 1972, Klee~\cite{Kl72 also studied cycle permutation graphs, where he referred to them as ``generalised prisms''.}
elements.sent-2411-010: He was interested in the hamiltonicity of cycle permutation graphs and asked for which orders there exist non-hamiltonian cycle permutation graphs.
elements.sent-2411-011: Using a computer search Klee determined that up to order $16$, the Petersen graph is the only non-hamiltonian cycle permutation graph and he partially solved the question by proving that for every order $n\equiv 2\bmod 4$ with $n\geq 18$ there exist non-hamiltonian cycle permutation graphs.
elements.sent-2411-012: In this manuscript, we finish this classification by completely characterising for which orders non-hamiltonian cycle permutation graphs exist.
elements.sent-2411-013: Cycle permutation graphs were also studied in the 80's by Shawe-Taylor and Pisanski, who asked questions about the girth of such graphs~\cite{PS82,PS81 .}
elements.sent-2411-014: Given a girth $g$, they construct cycle permutation graphs with girth $g$ or higher.
elements.sent-2411-015: We are also interested in cycle permutation graphs which are snarks, i.e.\ non-$3$-edge colourable.
elements.sent-2411-016: In this case we call them permutation snarks.
elements.sent-2411-017: We note that in some definitions, snarks are required to be cyclically $4$-edge-connected and/or of girth at least $5$, however, this is always the case for cycle permutation graphs of order $n > 6$.
elements.sent-2411-018: A graph is cyclically $k$-edge connected if the removal of fewer than $k$ edges cannot separate the graph into two components each containing a cycle and its girth is the length of a shortest cycle.
elements.sent-2411-019: Note that a cycle permutation graph with a cycle of length $4$ is always hamiltonian and hamiltonian graphs cannot be snarks.
elements.sent-2411-020: Snarks are particularly interesting since for a lot of open conjectures it can be shown that if the conjecture is false, the smallest possible counterexamples are snarks.
elements.sent-2411-021: This has amongst others been proven for the Cycle Double Cover conjecture~\cite{Se79,Sz73 and Tutte’s 5-flow conjecture~\cite{Tu54 .}}
elements.sent-2411-022: A better understanding of the class of snarks can therefore lead to a better understanding of many long-standing open conjectures.
elements.sent-2411-023: Permutation snarks in particular are of interest as their additional structure makes them natural candidates for investigation and this structure in turn allows us to study this class computationally up to significantly higher orders.
elements.sent-2411-024: It is well known that the Petersen graph is a permutation snark.
elements.sent-2411-025: In 1997 Zhang conjectured that it is the only permutation snark which is cyclically $5$-edge-connected~\cite{Zh97 .}
elements.sent-2411-026: This conjecture was refuted by Brinkmann, Goedgebeur, H\"agglund and Markstr\"om~\cite{BGHM13 in 2013, when they developed a new generation algorithm for cubic graphs and snarks and generated all snarks up to 36 vertices and determined that there are 12 cyclically $5$-edge-connected permutation snarks on $34$ vertices.}
elements.sent-2411-027: They obtained counts for permutation snarks by using a filter approach, which given an input graph (given by the generation algorithm) determines whether or not it is a permutation snark.
elements.sent-2411-028: However, this method is not very efficient as in practice very few cubic graphs are cycle permutation graphs and even fewer are permutation snarks.
elements.sent-2411-029: For example on order $32$ only $0.24\%$ of the cubic graphs of girth at least~$4$ (the minimum girth of a cycle permutation graph of order $> 6$) are cycle permutation graphs.
elements.sent-2411-030: We improve the result by Brinkmann et al.\ by determining all permutation snarks up to order $46$, yielding many more counterexamples to Zhang's conjecture.
elements.sent-2411-031: We have developed two specialised algorithms for the exhaustive generation of all pairwise non-isomorphic cycle permutation graphs of a given order.
elements.sent-2411-032: The first uses the canonical construction path method~\cite{Mc98 to make sure no isomorphic copies are output.}
elements.sent-2411-033: The second is based on orderly generation~\cite{Fa78,Re78 and is faster, but only takes care of some of the most common isomorphs.}
elements.sent-2411-034: The latter is most useful when the exact number of cycle permutation graphs is not relevant or when the number of output graphs is small enough so that removing isomorphs afterwards is feasible.
elements.sent-2411-035: We describe both approaches in Section~\ref{sec:algorithm .}
elements.sent-2411-036: Their implementations can be found on GitHub~\cite{GR24 .}
elements.sent-2411-037: Moreover, we extended both of these algorithms in order to restrict the search to non-hamiltonian cycle permutation graphs or graphs with a given lower bound on the girth in an efficient way.
elements.sent-2411-038: In 2019, Má\v{c ajová and \v{S koviera~\cite{MS19 gave three methods for constructing permutation snarks and used them to provide permutation snarks of cyclic connectivity $4$ and $5$ for every large enough order $n\equiv 2\bmod 8$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-039: They mention that no permutation snark of order $n\equiv 6\bmod 8$ is known and prove that such a smallest permutation snark -- if it exists -- must by cyclically $5$-edge-connected.
elements.sent-2411-040: The rest of this paper is organised as follows.
elements.sent-2411-041: In Section~\ref{sec:algorithm , we explain how the algorithms work and prove their correctness.}
elements.sent-2411-042: In Section~\ref{sec:results , we use the algorithm to obtain counts for cycle permutation graphs as well as non-hamiltonian cycle permutation graphs and permutation snarks.}
elements.sent-2411-043: For the latter, our algorithm allows us to generate all permutation snarks up to order $46$.
elements.sent-2411-044: We also increase lower bounds for interesting permutation snarks such as the minimum order for a permutation snark of order $n\equiv 6\bmod 8$, which was also posed as an open problem in~\cite{BGHM13 .}
elements.sent-2411-045: In Section~\ref{sec:orders , we finish the characterisation of the orders for which non-hamiltonian cycle permutation graphs exist, solving one of the questions by Klee~\cite{Kl72 .}}
elements.sent-2411-046: In this section, we will present our algorithms for exhaustively generating all cycle permutation graphs of a given order $n$.
elements.sent-2411-047: The basic idea is a backtracking approach, where we start from two cycles of length $n/2$ connected by one edge and add edges between the cycles in all necessary ways.
elements.sent-2411-048: Clearly, this generates all cycle permutation graphs.
elements.sent-2411-049: However, such an approach will give a lot of isomorphic copies.
elements.sent-2411-050: One way solve this is to keep track of all intermediate graphs and to stop adding edges whenever a graph isomorphic to a previously generated graph is found.
elements.sent-2411-051: However, this approach is very memory intensive.
elements.sent-2411-052: For example if one generates all cycle permutation graphs of order $28$ using this approach, approximately $160$ gigabytes of memory is needed.
elements.sent-2411-053: Therefore, we will use a different approach to avoid the generation of isomorphic copies.
elements.sent-2411-054: For our first algorithm (see Section~\ref{subsec:ccpm ), we will use the canonical construction path method introduced by McKay~\cite{Mc98 , which (if used correctly) guarantees that every graph is generated exactly once without having to store (and compare) all graphs in the memory during the generation process.}}
elements.sent-2411-055: For our second algorithm (see Section~\ref{subsec:orderly ), we reject isomorphisms using a method based on orderly generation~\cite{Fa78,Re78 , which was first used by Rozenfel\textprime d~\cite{Ro73 for generating strongly regular graphs.}}}
elements.sent-2411-056: One labelling in each isomorphism class is determined to be canonical and only these labellings will be accepted by the algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-057: For efficiency's sake, we tolerate that some isomorphic graphs have multiple ``canonical'' labellings, which might lead to isomorphic graphs being output.
elements.sent-2411-058: Again, in this case, every graph is generated at least once without having to store any graphs in memory.
elements.sent-2411-059: In the next subsections we describe these algorithms in more detail and prove their correctness.
elements.sent-2411-060: Our implementations of both algorithms can be found on GitHub~\cite{GR24 and in Appendix~\ref{app:correctness_tests we describe how we extensively tested the correctness of the implementations in various ways.}}
elements.sent-2411-061: An expansion is an operation which constructs a larger graph from a given smaller one.
elements.sent-2411-062: The reverse operation is called a reduction.
elements.sent-2411-063: To use the canonical construction path method we will need to define a canonical reduction which is unique up to isomorphism and its inverse operation will then be the canonical expansion.
elements.sent-2411-064: Two expansions applied to the same graph are equivalent if they lead to isomorphic graphs.
elements.sent-2411-065: This gives us, for each graph to which we apply expansions, classes of equivalent expansions.
elements.sent-2411-066: When applying an expansion we need to determine whether or not we will accept the newly generated graph and keep adding edges to it or if we discard it and choose another edge to add in the smaller graph.
elements.sent-2411-067: In order to avoid isomorphic copies, we should accept every non-isomorphic intermediate graph exactly once.
elements.sent-2411-068: This can be done by following these two rules:.
elements.sent-2411-069: Only accept a graph if it was obtained by canonical expansion.
elements.sent-2411-070: For every graph $G$ to which expansions will be applied, only perform one expansion from each equivalence class of expansions.
elements.sent-2411-071: In our case there is only one expansion operation, that is: adding an edge between pairs of specific vertices of degree $2$.
elements.sent-2411-072: Consider a subcubic graph $G$ of order $n$ which is a spanning subgraph, i.e.\ containing all vertices, of a cycle permutation graph.
elements.sent-2411-073: The $2$-factors of this graph consisting of two chordless cycles of length $n/2$ will be called permutation $2$-factors.
elements.sent-2411-074: Let $C_1$ and $C_2$ be the two induced cycles of a permutation $2$-factor $F$.
elements.sent-2411-075: We say $F$ is consecutive if the vertices of degree $3$ on $C_1$ induce a path (or a cycle) or if the vertices of degree $3$ on $C_2$ induce a path (or a cycle).
elements.sent-2411-076: Let $F$ be a consecutive permutation $2$-factor with induced cycles $C_1$ and $C_2$.
elements.sent-2411-077: For $i\in\{1,2\}$, if the degree $3$ vertices on $C_i$ induce a path $P$, let $S_i$ be the set of vertices of degree $2$ which are adjacent to $P$ on $C_i$ or let $S_i$ be empty otherwise.
elements.sent-2411-078: We consider a pair of vertices eligible if one of the vertices lies in such a set $S_i$ and the other is a vertex of degree $2$ on $C_{3-i}$.
elements.sent-2411-079: We will apply the expansion to any eligible pair of vertices for any consecutive permutation $2$-factor of our graph.
elements.sent-2411-080: Note, that the algorithm would also work if we add an edge between any pair of degree $2$ vertices in which one lies on $C_1$ and the other on $C_2$.
elements.sent-2411-081: However, restricting the algorithm to eligible pairs drastically reduces the amount of times the recursive algorithm branches and hence makes the implementation a lot more efficient.
elements.sent-2411-082: High level pseudocode for the recursive method of the algorithm can be found in Algorithm~\ref{alg:recursive_method .}
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Expand($G$)} \label{alg:recursive_method} \begin{algorithmic} \If{$G$ is cubic} \State Output $G$ \EndIf \State Determine all eligible pairs of vertices. \State Determine the orbits of all such pairs. \ForAll{eligible pairs $(u,v)$} \State // Cf.\ Rule~\ref{rule:CCPM_2} of the canonical construction path method. \If{$(u,v)$ is the representative of its orbit} \If{adding edge $uv$ to $G$ is a canonical expansion} // Cf.\ Rule~\ref{rule:CCPM_1}. \State Expand($G+uv$) \EndIf \EndIf \EndFor \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-083: We now explain the algorithm in more detail.
elements.sent-2411-084: Suppose we want to generate the cycle permutation graphs of order $n$.
elements.sent-2411-085: We start with $G_0$ consisting of two cycles $C_1 = v_0v_1\ldots v_{n/2-1}$ and $C_2 = w_0w_1\ldots w_{n/2-1}$ and the edge $v_0w_0$.
elements.sent-2411-086: Note that $C_1\cup C_2$ forms a consecutive permutation $2$-factor.
elements.sent-2411-087: Let $G$ be a (sub)cubic supergraph of $G_0$ of order $n$ which contains a consecutive permutation $2$-factor.
elements.sent-2411-088: If $G$ is cubic then it is a cycle permutation graph.
elements.sent-2411-089: It should be output and the recursion backtracks.
elements.sent-2411-090: If not, we determine all consecutive permutation $2$-factors in order to find the eligible pairs of vertices.
elements.sent-2411-091: In practice, we keep track of all consecutive permutation $2$-factors dynamically for efficiency reasons.
elements.sent-2411-092: More specifically: after adding an edge, we remove those which have become non-consecutive or are no longer permutation $2$-factors and search for consecutive permutation $2$-factors containing the newly added edge.
elements.sent-2411-093: While constructing the induced cycles of such a $2$-factor they are stored in a bitset, so that we can use bit operations to determine efficiently whether or not they have chords.
elements.sent-2411-094: This search for new consecutive permutation $2$-factors containing the most recently added edge is one of the bottlenecks of the algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-095: Given all consecutive permutation $2$-factors, it is straightforward to determine all eligible pairs of vertices.
elements.sent-2411-096: We then need to determine the orbits of these pairs in order to only add an edge between one of the vertex pairs of each orbit, since all pairs in the same orbit would lead to isomorphic graphs.
elements.sent-2411-097: To obtain these orbits we use the program nauty~\cite{MP14 for determining all generators of the automorphism group of $G$.}
elements.sent-2411-098: The orbits are then determined using a union-find algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-099: We choose the representative of each orbit to be the first vertex pair of that orbit we encountered while searching for eligible vertex pairs.
elements.sent-2411-100: Once we have determined that an eligible vertex pair $(u,v)$ is the representative of its orbit, we need to determine whether its addition to $G$ will be a canonical expansion.
elements.sent-2411-101: In practice, we do this by looking at the graph $G+uv$ and analysing its reducible edges.
elements.sent-2411-102: These are the edges $e$ for which $G + uv - e$ contains a consecutive permutation $2$-factor.
elements.sent-2411-103: These are however easily obtained once one knows all consecutive permutation $2$-factors of $G + uv$.
elements.sent-2411-104: Other edges are not reducible and can be ignored since removing them does not give us a graph which needs to be generated.
elements.sent-2411-105: In order to determine whether or not we should accept $G + uv$ we need to define a canonical reduction which is unique up to isomorphism.
elements.sent-2411-106: The edge whose removal yields the canonical reduction will be the canonical edge.
elements.sent-2411-107: To this end, we assign a $10$-tuple $(x_0, \ldots, x_9)$ to each reducible edge and let the canonical edge be the one with the lexicographically maximal value for this $10$-tuple.
elements.sent-2411-108: For a reducible edge $e=ab$, the values $x_0,\ldots, x_7$ are invariants of increasing discriminating power and cost, determined empirically.
elements.sent-2411-110: We note that while the discriminating power is negligible for the later invariants, they are necessary for the efficiency of variants of this program, such as the generation of non-hamiltonian cycle permutation graphs or cycle permutation graphs with girth restrictions.. See Section~\ref{sec:results .}
elements.sent-2411-111: Moreover, their presence here does not noticeably increase the running time of the algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-112: While the above values are invariant under isomorphism, their values could be the same for non-isomorphic graphs.
elements.sent-2411-113: Therefore we define $\{x_8, x_9\}$ to be the lexicographically largest label of an edge which is in the same edge orbit as $e$ in the canonical labelling of the graph.
elements.sent-2411-114: We again use nauty~\cite{MP14 for determining this canonical labelling of the graph.}
elements.sent-2411-115: Once such an invariant is able to show that our added edge $uv$ is not canonical, for example if there is a reducible edge with fewer vertices at distance at most $2$, then we can reject the expansion immediately and do not need to compute the remaining invariants (including the expensive call to nauty).
elements.sent-2411-116: Similarly, the invariants can be sufficient to determine if our added edge is the only reducible edge with maximum value for the tuple and hence it is canonical.
elements.sent-2411-117: The expansion can then immediately be accepted without computing the remaining invariants.
elements.sent-2411-118: As an example, on order $24$, invariants $x_0,\ldots, x_7$ are sufficient to discriminate the canonical edge in $94.64\%$ of the cases.
elements.sent-2411-119: If we were to only consider $x_8$ and $x_9$ for this order, this would slow down the total computation by a factor of $5$.
elements.sent-2411-120: One of the bottlenecks of our algorithm is the search for new consecutive permutation $2$-factors containing the most recently added edge.
elements.sent-2411-121: This needs to happen after adding an eligible non-edge $e$ in order to determine all reducible edges and find out whether the addition of $e$ was a canonical expansion.
elements.sent-2411-122: We try to avoid this as much as possible.

elements.sent-2411-166: In this section we will present an alternative algorithm based on orderly generation which we called weak orderly generation.
elements.sent-2411-167: With this method we will ``ignore'' the fact that multiple permutation $2$-factors can exist.

elements.sent-2411-170: Orderly generation defines a canonically labelled object for each isomorphism class and generates only that one for each isomorphism class.
elements.sent-2411-171: Ignoring the fact that a cycle permutation graph can have multiple permutation $2$-factors, our problem lends itself well to orderly generation and our recursive method needs to branch a lot less, which is why it is a lot faster than the canonical construction path method.
elements.sent-2411-172: The only caveat is that multiple canonically labelled objects can exist for a single isomorphism class, which is why we call it weak orderly generation.
elements.sent-2411-173: We will use the notation $[x]:= \{0,1,\ldots, x-1\}$ for the set of the first $x$ integers (including $0$).
elements.sent-2411-175: A partial $k$-permutation of size $l$ is a permutation from $[l]$ to a subset $S$ of $[k]$ of order $l$.
elements.sent-2411-176: When $S = [l]$, we say the partial permutation is restricted.
elements.sent-2411-177: Let $S$ be a subset of $[l]$, then we define $\pi(S):=\{\pi(s)|s\in S\}\subset [k]$.
elements.sent-2411-178: We can represent a subcubic graph $G$ containing a consecutive permutation $2$-factor $F$ by its order $n$ and a (partial) $n/2$-permutation.

elements.section-2411-004: Orders of non-hamiltonian cycle permutation graphs
elements.sent-2411-277: In his 1972 paper~\cite{Kl72 , Klee studied two questions involving the non-hamiltonicity of cycle permutation graphs, attributed to Ralph Willoughby.}
elements.sent-2411-278: The first one asks which permutation graphs admit a hamiltonian cycle.
elements.sent-2411-279: The second asks for which orders $n$ there exist non-hamiltonian cycle permutation graphs.
elements.sent-2411-280: Klee answered this second question partially by proving that for all $n\equiv 2\bmod 4$ there is a non-hamiltonian cycle permutation graph if and only if $n$ is neither $6$ nor $14$.
elements.sent-2411-281: We complete the characterisation of the orders for which non-hamiltonian cycle permutation graphs exist and thereby solve Klee's second question.
elements.sent-2411-282: We use Klee's notation, but opt for $0$-indexing of the permutations.
elements.sent-2411-283: One can define cycle permutation graphs of order $n=2k$ given a $k$-permutation.
elements.sent-2411-284: Let $G(\pi)$ be the cubic graph consisting of two disjoint $k$-cycles $x_0x_1\ldots x_{k-1}$ and $y_0y_1\ldots y_{k-1}$ and edges $x_iy_{\pi(i)}$ for $0\leq i\leq k-1$.
elements.sent-2411-285: Let $G'(\pi)$ be the subgraph of $G(\pi)$ where we have removed the edges $x_{k-1 x_0$ and $y_{k-1 y_0$.}}
elements.sent-2411-286: An $E$-path in the graph $G'(\pi)$ is a path for which its ends are both in the set $E=\{x_0, x_{k-1 , y_0, y_{k-1}\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-287: A hamiltonian $E$-pair is a pair of disjoint $E$-paths containing all vertices of $G'(\pi)$.
elements.sent-2411-288: The permutation $\pi$ is called good if it either has a hamiltonian $E$-path with endpoints $x_i$ and $y_j$ for $i,j\in\{0,k-1\}$ or if it has a hamiltonian $E$-pair such that one of the disjoint paths has endpoints $x_i$ and $y_j$ for $i,j\in\{0,k-1\}$ and the other has endpoints $x_{k-1-i , y_{k-1-j}$.}
elements.sent-2411-290: If $\pi$ is a $k$-permutation with $\pi(k-1) = k-1$, then we can naturally restrict $\pi$ from $[k]$ to $[k-1]$.
elements.sent-2411-291: We denote this permutation by $\overline{\pi}$, i.e.\ $\overline{\pi (i) = \pi(i)$ for $i\in [k-1]$.}
elements.sent-2411-292: Given $m$ $k_i$-permutations, for $0\leq i < m$, we can concatenate them to obtain a new $(\sum_{i=0 ^{m-1 k_i)$-permutation $\pi:= (\pi_0,\ldots, \pi_{m-1 )$, where \begin{equation}\pi(i) = \sum_{j=0}^{l-1} k_j + \pi_i(i - \sum_{j=0}^{l-1} k_j),  for  \sum_{j=0}^{l-1} k_j \le i < \sum_{j=0}^{l} k_j.\end{equation}.}}}

elements.sent-2411-331: We present an algorithm for the efficient generation of all pairwise non-isomorphic cycle permutation graphs, i.e.\ cubic graphs with a $2$-factor consisting of two chordless cycles, non-hamiltonian cycle permutation graphs and permutation snarks, i.e.\ cycle permutation graphs that do not admit a $3$-edge-colouring.
elements.sent-2411-332: They construct cycle permutation graphs with an arbitrarily large girth.
elements.sent-2411-333: A graph is cyclically $k$-edge-connected if the removal of fewer than $k$ edges cannot separate the graph into two components each containing a cycle and its girth is the length of a shortest cycle.
elements.sent-2411-334: This has amongst others been proven for the Cycle Double Cover conjecture~\cite{Se79_2,Sz73 and Tutte’s 5-flow conjecture~\cite{Tu54 .}}
elements.sent-2411-335: In 1997, Zhang conjectured that it is the only permutation snark which is cyclically $5$-edge-connected~\cite{Zh97 .}
elements.sent-2411-336: They obtained counts for permutation snarks by using a filter approach, which given an input graph (given by the generation algorithm), determines whether or not it is a permutation snark.
elements.sent-2411-337: In 2019, Má\v{c ajová and \v{S koviera~\cite{MS19 gave three methods for constructing permutation snarks and used them to provide permutation snarks of cyclic connectivity $4$ and $5$ for sufficiently large order $n\equiv 2\pmod 8$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-338: They mention that no permutation snark of order $n\equiv 6\pmod 8$ is known (an open problem posed by Brinkmann et al.~\cite{BGHM13 ) and prove that such a smallest permutation snark -- if it exists -- must by cyclically $5$-edge-connected.}
elements.sent-2411-339: Note that cycle permutation graphs of order $n\equiv 0\pmod 4$ are trivially $3$-edge-colourable since the two cycles in the 2-factor have even length.
elements.sent-2411-340: The first algorithm is based on orderly generation~\cite{Fa78,Re78 .}
elements.sent-2411-341: When interested in non-hamiltonian cycle permutation graphs or permutation snarks, we slightly adapt the algorithm in order to only take care of the most common isomorphisms.
elements.sent-2411-342: This speeds up the algorithm significantly at the cost of outputting some isomorphic copies of the same graph.
elements.sent-2411-343: We also describe a second algorithm using the canonical construction path method~\cite{Mc98 .}
elements.sent-2411-344: Its implementation is slower than the approach using orderly generation, however, we use it as a verification for the correctness of our implementation of the first algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-345: The orderly generation approach is described in Section~\ref{sec:algorithm , while the canonical construction path approach is described in Appendix~\ref{app:ccpm .}}
elements.sent-2411-346: Variations of these algorithms allow to restrict the search to non-hamiltonian cycle permutation graphs, permutation snarks or graphs with a given lower bound on the girth in an efficient way.
elements.sent-2411-347: This allowed us to exhaustively generate all cycle permutation graphs and permutation snarks up to much higher orders.
elements.sent-2411-348: In Section~\ref{sec:results , we use the algorithms to obtain counts for cycle permutation graphs as well as non-hamiltonian cycle permutation graphs and permutation snarks.}
elements.sent-2411-349: In this section, we will present our algorithm for exhaustively generating all cycle permutation graphs of a given order $n$.
elements.sent-2411-350: The basic idea is a backtracking approach, where we start from two cycles of length $n/2$ connected by one edge and add edges between the cycles in all possible ways.
elements.sent-2411-351: In our algorithm (see Section~\ref{subsec:orderly ), we reject isomorphisms using a method based on orderly generation~\cite{Fa78,Re78 .}}
elements.sent-2411-352: One labelling in each isomorphism class is determined to be (strongly) canonical and only these labellings will be accepted by the algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-353: Then every graph is generated precisely once without having to store any graphs in memory.
elements.sent-2411-354: For efficiency's sake, in the case we want to generate non-hamiltonian cycle permutation graphs or permutation snarks, we tolerate that some isomorphic graphs have multiple ``canonical'' labellings, which might lead to isomorphic graphs being output.
elements.sent-2411-355: While this method can output isomorphic copies of the same graph, this approach is a lot faster as we can avoid performing certain expensive computations while still filtering the most common isomorphs.
elements.sent-2411-356: Moreover, since the number of graphs output in this way remains relatively small, it is feasible to obtain the exact number of graphs by filtering the isomorphic copies in a post-processing step.
elements.sent-2411-357: We now describe these algorithms in more detail and prove their correctness.
elements.sent-2411-358: A description of the algorithm using the canonical construction path method can be found in Appendix~\ref{app:ccpm .}
elements.sent-2411-359: Our implementation of the algorithms can be found on GitHub~\cite{GR24 and in Appendix~\ref{app:correctness_tests we describe how we extensively tested the correctness of the implementations in various ways.}}
elements.sent-2411-360: The idea of this method is based on orderly generation, which was independently introduced by Farad\v zev~\cite{Fa78 and Read~\cite{Re78 in 1978, but had already been used by Rozenfel'd~\cite{Ro73 in 1973 for the generation of strongly regular graphs.}}}
elements.sent-2411-361: Orderly generation defines a canonically labelled object for each isomorphism class and outputs only that one for each isomorphism class.
elements.sent-2411-362: As we will see, the problem of generating cycle permutation graphs lends itself well to orderly generation and is much faster than, for example, the canonical construction path method, which we describe in Appendix~\ref{app:ccpm .}
elements.sent-2411-364: Let $G$ be a subcubic graph of even order $n$, i.e.\ a graph in which all vertices have degree at most $3$.
elements.sent-2411-365: If $G$ contains two vertex-disjoint chordless cycles $C_1$ and $C_2$ of length $k:= n/2$, then $F := \{C_1, C_2\}$ is called a permutation $2$-factor of $G$, i.e.\ a $2$-factor consisting of chordless cycles $C_1$ and $C_2$.
elements.sent-2411-366: We call any edges of $G$ that have one endpoint in $C_1$ and one endpoint in $C_2$ spokes of $F$.
elements.sent-2411-367: If the vertices of degree $2$ in $G$ on one of the cycles induce a path, we call this cycle $consecutive$ and we call $F$ a consecutive permutation $2$-factor of $G$.
elements.sent-2411-368: We will represent subgraphs of cycle permutation graphs in the following way.
elements.sent-2411-369: Let $G$ be a subcubic graph containing a permutation $2$-factor $F$ with cycles $C_1$ and $C_2$.
elements.sent-2411-370: Let $C_1 = u_0u_1\ldots u_{k-1}$ and $C_2 = v_0v_1\ldots v_{k-1}$.
elements.sent-2411-371: Let $[x]:= \{0,1,\ldots, x-1\}$ for the set of the first $x$ integers\footnote{Note that this might deviate slightly from how this notation is typically used.. .}
elements.sent-2411-372: Then $G$ can be represented by the sequence $p = p_0, \ldots, p_{k-1}$ (also known as a partial permutation), where $p_i$ for $i\in [k]$ is given by \begin{equation}p: [k] \rightarrow [k] \cup \{{?}\}: i \mapsto  j, if $u_i$ is adjacent to $v_j$, ?, otherwise. \end{equation} We say $p$ represents $G$ via $F$.
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation}p: [k] \rightarrow [k] \cup \{{?}\}: i \mapsto  j, if $u_i$ is adjacent to $v_j$, ?, otherwise. \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-373: Note that $G$ and $F$ are not sufficient to determine $p$, we also need an assignment of the vertices of $F$ to the variables $u_i, v_i$, with $i\in[k]$.
elements.sent-2411-374: Therefore, $G$ can have many such sequences representing it even via the same permutation $2$-factor $F$.
elements.sent-2411-375: On the other hand, a sequence $p$ gives rise to a labelling of $G$.
elements.sent-2411-376: Label the vertices of $G$ from $0$ to $n-1$ such that if $i\in [k]$, then it has neighbours, $i-1$, $i+1$, taking indices modulo $k$, and $k + p(i)$, if $p(i)\neq {?}$.
elements.sent-2411-377: If $i\in [n]\setminus[k]$, then it has neighbours $i-1$, $i+1$, taking indices modulo $k$ and then adding $k$, and $j$ if there exists a $j\in [k]$ such that $p(j) = (i-k)$.
elements.sent-2411-378: In other words, if $p$ represents $G$ via $F$ with cycles $u_0\ldots u_{k-1}$ and $v_0\ldots v_{k-1}$, this labelling is obtained from the isomorphism mapping $u_i$ to $i$ and $v_i$ to $k+i$ for $i\in [k]$.
elements.sent-2411-379: We say this labelling, denoted by $G(p)$, is given by $p$.
elements.sent-2411-380: We call a sequence $p$ (strongly) canonical if it is lexicographically smallest among all sequences representing $G$, where $i < {?}$ for all $i\in [k]$.
elements.sent-2411-381: We call it weakly canonical if it is lexicographically smallest among all such sequences representing $G$ via $F$ (but as we will see later, being weakly canonical is independent of $F$).
elements.sent-2411-382: We will use weak canonicity in Section~\ref{sec:non-ham .}
elements.algorithm-2411-003: \caption{Expand($p$, $l$)} \label{alg:recursive_method_orderly} \begin{algorithmic} \If{$l = k$} \State Output cubic graph represented by $p$. \EndIf \ForAll{$x\in [k]\setminus \operatorname{im}(p)$} \State Define $p'$ by $p'(i) = p(i)$ for $i\in [k]\setminus\{l\}$ and $p'(l) = x$. \If{$p'$ is canonical} \State Expand($p'$, $l+1$) \EndIf \EndFor \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-383: A rough outline of the recursive method without any optimisations or technical details can be found in Algorithm~\ref{alg:recursive_method_orderly by starting with $p = 0,{? , {? , \ldots, {?}$ and $l = 1$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-384: We denote the image of $p$ by $\operatorname{im (p)$.}
elements.sent-2411-385: We start with $G_0$ consisting of two disjoint cycles $C_1 = u_0u_1\ldots u_{k-1}$ and $C_2=v_0v_1\ldots v_{k-1}$ and the edge $u_0v_0$.
elements.sent-2411-386: Note that this is a subgraph of any cycle permutation graph.
elements.sent-2411-387: Now let $G$ be a (sub)cubic supergraph of $G_0$ of order $n$ whose labelling is represented by $p^l$ with $\lvert \operatorname{im (p^l)\setminus\{{?}\}\rvert = l$.}
elements.sent-2411-388: If not, we recursively add edges from $u_l$ to $v_i$ with $i\in[k] \setminus \operatorname{im (p)$.}
elements.sent-2411-389: Adding such an edge $u_lv_i$ gives us a new graph whose labelling is represented by a sequence $p' = p_0, \ldots, p_{l-1 , i, {? , \ldots, {?}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-390: We must now decide to continue with $p'$ if it is canonical, i.e.\ lexicographically minimal among all sequences representing $G$, or to discard it.
elements.sent-2411-391: We now describe how to determine canonicity of a given sequence $p$ representing a graph $G$.
elements.sent-2411-392: We can generate every sequence representing $G$ using three operations on these sequences as long as we have such a sequence representing $G$ via every permutation $2$-factor $F$ of $G$.
elements.sent-2411-393: We use the notation $\bmod\ k$ as a mapping sending an integer $i_1$ to an integer $i_2\in \{0,\ldots, k-1\}$ such that $i_2\equiv i_1\bmod k$.
elements.sent-2411-394: The first operation is a rotation $T: p\mapsto q$ such that $q(i) = p((i+1)\bmod k)$.
elements.sent-2411-395: It corresponds to an isomorphism $\phi_T$ from $G(p)$ to $G(q)$ sending vertices $i\in [k]$ to $(i+1)\bmod k$ and the remaining vertices to themselves.
elements.sent-2411-396: This can be thought of as rotating the labels of the first cycle.
elements.sent-2411-397: The second operation is a reflection $R: p\mapsto q$ such that $q(i) = p(k - 1 - i)$ for $i\in [k]$.
elements.sent-2411-398: It corresponds to an isomorphism $\phi_R$ from $G(p)$ to $G(q)$ sending vertices $i\in[k]$ to $k-1-i$ and the remaining vertices to themselves.
elements.sent-2411-399: This can be seen as reflecting the first cycle.
elements.sent-2411-400: The third operation is an inversion, $I: p\mapsto q$ such that $q(i) = p^{-1 (i)$ if $i\in \operatorname{im (p)$ and ${?}$ otherwise.}}
elements.sent-2411-401: It corresponds to an isomorphism $\phi_I$ from $G(p)$ to $G(q)$ sending the vertices $i\in[k]$ to $k+i$ and vice versa.
elements.sent-2411-402: This can be seen as swapping the cycles.
elements.sent-2411-403: Let $G$ be a (sub)cubic graph containing a permutation $2$-factor.
elements.sent-2411-404: Then, any sequence representing $G$ via a permutation $2$-factor $F$ can be obtained from any other sequence representing $G$ via $F$ by applying some composition of the above three operations $T$, $R$ and $I$.
elements.sent-2411-405: Let $p$ be a sequence representing $G$ via $F$ and let $q$ be another such sequence.
elements.sent-2411-406: There exists an isomorphism $\phi$ between $G(p)$ and $G(q)$.
elements.sent-2411-407: We note that $\phi$ maps the cycles given by $0,\ldots, k-1$ and $k,\ldots, n-1$ to themselves or to each other.
elements.sent-2411-408: There are $2k$ ways a cycle can be mapped to another cycle, namely $k$ choices to map the first element and a choice of direction.
elements.sent-2411-409: Since we are mapping two cycles and might swap these, this yields $8k^2$ options.
elements.sent-2411-410: It follows that $\phi$ is some composition of $\phi_T$, $\phi_R$ and $\phi_I$.
elements.sent-2411-411: We note that a rotation or reflection of the second cycle can be obtained by swapping them first.
elements.sent-2411-412: Since these isomorphisms correspond to $T$, $R$ and $I$, the result follows.
elements.sent-2411-413: Therefore, in order to determine weak canonicity of a sequence $p$, it suffices to compare it to any other of the at most $8k^2$ sequences obtained by applying any composition of $T$, $R$ and $I$ to it.
elements.sent-2411-414: Hence, this does not depend on the given $2$-factor via which it represents $G$.
elements.sent-2411-415: We can now check (strong) canonicity of $p$ as follows.
elements.sent-2411-416: For any permutation $2$-factor $F$ and a sequence $p^F$ representing $G$ via $F$, check if $p$ is lexicographically smaller than all rotations of $C_1$ and or $C_2$ of $F$.
elements.sent-2411-417: Then check all rotations when the first cycle of $F$ is reflected ($R(p^F)$), the second is reflected ($I\circ R\circ I(p^F)$), the cycles are swapped ($I(p^F)$) or any combination of these.
elements.sent-2411-418: Once a sequence smaller then $p$ is found we can prune the search.
elements.sent-2411-419: We note that in practice, we use a slightly different notion of canonicity based on the difference lists of the sequences representing $G$ and we use many optimisations such as not checking sequences for which it can immediately be determined that they are larger.
elements.sent-2411-420: We also use optimisations that allow us to check many of these sequences in constant time.
elements.sent-2411-421: See Appendix~\ref{app:orderly_optimisations for some details on these optimisations.}
elements.sent-2411-422: For what follows and the proof of the algorithm's correctness we use the following two results.
elements.sent-2411-423: Let $G$ be a subcubic graph containing a permutation $2$-factor.
elements.sent-2411-424: Every permutation $2$-factor with cycles $C_1$ and $C_2$ will have the same number of degree $2$ vertices in $C_1$ and $C_2$ (namely half of all degree 2 vertices in $G$).
elements.sent-2411-425: Then every permutation $2$-factor of $G$ will necessarily be consecutive and partition the degree $2$ vertices in the same way.

elements.sent-2411-575: Suppose we encounter $p$ in our algorithm and its first $m$ entries are filled, i.e.\ not equal to ${?}$, and the remaining entries are ${?}$.
elements.sent-2411-576: We use the knowledge that $p\vert_{m-1}$ was canonical to speed up some of the computations of determining whether $p$ is canonical.
elements.sent-2411-577: Let $d:= d(p)$ be the difference list of $p$ and let $F$ be the natural $2$-factor obtained from the labelling $G(p)$.
elements.sent-2411-578: When computing whether or not $d$ is canonical, we first check if it is weakly canonical before checking whether it is lexicographically smaller than sequences representing $G$ via a different permutation $2$-factor.
elements.sent-2411-579: In order to check its weak canonicity, we need to check the rotations of eight lists, namely the $k$ rotations of $d$, the $k$ rotations of $d$ in the opposite direction, the $k$ rotations of the list where we subtract each element of $d$ from $k$, the $k$ rotations of taking both the opposite direction and subtracting and the $k$ rotations of the four previous lists, but for $d(I(p))$ instead of $d$.
elements.sent-2411-580: Note that $I(p)$ corresponds to swapping the cycles of $F$.
elements.sent-2411-581: A naive approach would linearly check for each of these lists whether or not $d$ is lexicographically smaller.
elements.sent-2411-582: However, for each of the lists we dynamically keep track which subsequences whose next element is ${?}$ are also a prefix of $d$.
elements.sent-2411-583: This allows us to often determine canonicity using only one comparison for each such subsequence.
elements.sent-2411-584: On average, there seem to be around 2 subsequences that match with $d$ distributed over the 8 lists, so in practice the canonicity test is almost free.
elements.sent-2411-585: We illustrate this with an example.
elements.sent-2411-587: Suppose we want to compare $d(p)$ to all of its rotations.
elements.sent-2411-588: Since the final $4$ of $d(p)$ was a ${?}$ in the previous iteration (i.e.\ for $d(p\vert_{5 )$), we have stored ``$2$'' as a subsequence which is a prefix of $d(p)$.}

elements.sent-2411-592: Since the sequence ``$2$" has length $1$, we compare the second element of $d(p)$, i.e.\ $4$ to the new $4$.
elements.sent-2411-593: If it were larger, then there is a rotation yielding a lexicographically smaller sequence than $d(p)$, if it were smaller, then this rotation is not lexicographically smaller and with the new element we cannot extend this subsequence to a new one which is still a prefix.
